name: Tomato___Early_blight
cause: Fungal infection caused by Alternaria solani, common in warm and humid conditions.
knowledge: Prune lower leaves to prevent spores from splashing up from the soil.
Rotate crops every 2-3 years to avoid soil-borne fungus buildup.
Target older leaves first as they are most susceptible.

name: Tomato___Late_blight
cause: Caused by Phytophthora infestans, spreads rapidly in cool and wet conditions.
knowledge: Destroy all infected plant debris; do not compost it as the pathogen can survive.
Monitor weather; high humidity and temperatures between 15-20°C trigger rapid outbreaks.
Late blight can kill a whole field in days; early detection is critical.

name: Tomato___Bacterial_spot
cause: Bacterial infection caused by Xanthomonas species, spread through water splash and contaminated tools.
knowledge: Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of the plant.
Sanitize garden tools with alcohol or bleach after working with infected plants.
Bacteria can enter through tiny wounds or natural pores (stomata) in the leaves.

name: Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_mite
cause: Caused by spider mites that suck plant sap, common in hot and dry conditions.
knowledge: Check the undersides of leaves for fine silk webbing.
Increase humidity around plants; mites thrive in dusty, dry environments.
Natural predators like ladybugs can help control mite populations.

name: Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus
cause: Viral disease transmitted by whiteflies, causing leaf curling and yellowing.
knowledge: Focus on whitefly control; the virus cannot be cured once the plant is infected.
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and catch whiteflies.
Remove weeds around the garden, as they often host the virus and the flies.

name: Tomato___Leaf_Mold
cause: Fungal disease caused by high humidity and poor air circulation.
knowledge: Common in greenhouses; ensure fans and vents are used for air movement.
The fungus appears as pale green spots on top and olive-colored mold underneath.
Keep relative humidity below 85% to stop the fungus from spreading.

name: Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot
cause: Fungal infection caused by Septoria lycopersici, spreads through water splash.
knowledge: Does not usually affect the fruit, but leaf loss can lead to sunscald on tomatoes.
Use mulch (straw or plastic) to create a barrier between the soil and the leaves.
Wash hands after handling infected plants to prevent spreading spores.

name: Tomato___Target_Spot
cause: Fungal disease causing circular target-like spots, common in humid environments.
knowledge: Look for concentric rings inside the leaf spots, resembling a bullseye.
Maintain adequate spacing between plants to allow leaves to dry quickly.
Pathogen can survive on old tomato stakes; disinfect equipment annually.

name: Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus
cause: Viral disease spread through contact, tools, and infected seeds.
knowledge: Smokers should wash hands before touching plants; the virus can exist in tobacco products.
Plant certified disease-free seeds or resistant varieties (labeled 'T').
Immediately rogue (pull out and burn) any plant showing mottled green patterns.

name: Tomato___healthy
cause: No disease detected. The plant is healthy.
knowledge: Continue regular monitoring once a week for early signs of pests.
Maintain a consistent watering schedule to prevent blossom end rot.
Healthy soil organic matter boosts the plant's natural immunity.
